quote: "I cut it off, brother," said Egg. "I didn't want to look like you."
why: Egg's own confession that he cut his own hair to conceal his Targaryen coloring from Aerion.

quote: They call him Daeron the Drunken, though not in his father's hearing.
why: The drunk lordling Dunk meets at the inn is revealed as Prince Daeron, Maekar's heir, concealed under a common nickname.

quote: It's short for Aegon. My brother Aemon named me Egg. He's off at the Citadel now, learning to be a maester. And Daeron sometimes calls me Egg as well, and so do my sisters.
why: Squire 'Egg' reveals his true name is Prince Aegon Targaryen, naming his siblings.

quote: He frowned for a moment, and then blurted out, "Ser Duncan the Tall." He was tall, no one could dispute that, and it sounded puissant.
why: Dunk invents his knightly name and identity on the spot, having no house of his own.

quote: "I did not think to enter the lists at Ashford, my lord, so I brought no armor. My son was good enough to lend me his."
why: Baelor Breakspear reveals himself, fighting the trial of seven in his son Valarr's borrowed black armor, briefly mistaken for Valarr.

quote: "The maesters shaved it off." Suddenly selfconscious, the boy pulled up the hood of his dark brown cloak, covering his head.
why: Egg's shaved head is the concrete concealment detail hiding his dragon-bright Targaryen hair — the mystery-engine of the novella, here still attached to Dunk's POV before the reveal.

quote: The boy hesitated. "Egg," he said.
why: The stableboy gives only his nickname 'Egg,' withholding his true identity as Prince Aegon Targaryen.

quote: "Not clever enough?" The speaker wore a black cloak bordered in scarlet satin, but underneath was raiment bright as flame, all reds and yellows and golds. Slim and straight as a dirk, though only of middling height, he was near Dunk's own age. Curls of silver-gold hair framed a face sculpted and imperious; high brow and sharp cheekbones, straight nose, pale smooth skin without blemish. His eyes were a deep violet color.
why: Arrogant unnamed princeling at the stables (later identified as Aerion Brightflame) described by his Targaryen coloring, identity withheld in this scene.

quote: He had blue eyes, Dunk saw, very dark, almost purple. His bald head made them seem huge, somehow.
why: Egg's eyes described as almost-purple — the Targaryen coloring hint left unconnected in this unit.

quote: Dunk handed it over. "I am called Ser Duncan the Tall."
why: Dunk states his invented knightly name to Tanselle.

quote: "No they won't." Egg moved closer. "If they do, they'll answer to my father. And my uncle as well. Let go of him, I said. Wate, Yorkel, you know me. Do as I say."
why: Egg invokes his father (Maekar) and uncle (Baelor) to command Aerion's men-at-arms off Dunk — a princely-authority reveal.

quote: It was him shaved my head. He knew my father would send men hunting us.
why: Egg explains Daeron shaved his head to hide his silver-gold Targaryen hair while the brothers fled Summerhall.

quote: It was your squire who came to me. The boy, Aegon.
why: Dunk's squire 'Egg' is named in-text as Aegon by Lyonel Baratheon.

quote: "Me?" Dunk's mouth opened and closed and opened again. "Egg . . . Aegon, I mean, he is a good lad, but, Your Grace, I know you honor me, but . . . I am only a hedge knight."
why: Dunk stumbles over Egg's true name Aegon in front of Prince Maekar, confirming the identity to the reader in Dunk's own voice.

quote: He could vanquish Ser Duncan the Tall, but not Dunk of Flea Bottom.
why: Dunk's dual identity stated outright: the knightly persona 'Ser Duncan the Tall' versus the lowborn Flea Bottom alley-fighter underneath.

quote: We were the masters of dragons once, we Targaryens. Now they are all gone, but we remain.
why: Daeron states the Targaryen dynasty's lost dragon-mastery and the family's persistence after the dragons' extinction.

quote: She'd been a green female, small and stunted, her wings withered. None of her eggs had ever hatched.
why: Physical description of the last dragon Ser Arlan saw in King's Landing as a boy, the year before it died.

quote: Good King Daeron had four grown sons, three with sons of their own.
why: Establishes Daeron II's four sons (Baelor, Aerys, Rhaegel, Maekar) and their own sons, the succession generation at Ashford.

quote: "I have sent Aerion to Lys," he announced abruptly. "A few years in the Free Cities may change him for the better."
why: Prince Maekar banishes his son Aerion Brightflame to the Free Cities after the trial.

quote: My brother Aemon named me Egg. He's off at the Citadel now, learning to be a maester.
why: Establishes that Prince Aemon Targaryen (the future Maester Aemon) has already been sent to the Citadel to train as a maester.

quote: Some say King Aegon poisoned her," the old man would tell. "The third Aegon that would be, not King Daeron's father, but the one they named Dragonbane, or Aegon the Unlucky.
why: The rumor that Aegon III (the Dragonbane/the Unlucky) poisoned the last dragon.

quote: Ser Arlan had not ridden a tilt since the day he had been unhorsed by the Prince of Dragonstone in a tourney at Storm's End, many years before. "It is not every man who can boast that he broke seven lances against the finest knight in the Seven Kingdoms," he would say.
why: Ser Arlan's own tourney history: unhorsed by the Prince of Dragonstone at Storm's End, a defeat he wore as a point of pride.

quote: "Dragons," men called the coins, since they were stamped with the three-headed dragon of House Targaryen on one side. The other bore the likeness of the king. Two of the coins Henly gave him had King Daeron's face; the third was older, well worn, and showed a different man. His name was there under his head, but Dunk could not read the letters.
why: Targaryen dynastic coinage — 'dragons' stamped with the three-headed dragon and the reigning king's face; Dunk's illiteracy keeps the older king unnamed.

quote: I am speaking with Baelor Targaryen, Prince of Dragonstone, Hand of the King, and heir apparent to the Iron Throne of Aegon the Conqueror.
why: Baelor Breakspear's full titles and status as heir apparent, recognized by Dunk mid-conversation.

quote: He could only be Prince Maekar, the youngest of King Daeron's four sons.
why: Dunk identifies the pox-scarred, silver-bearded prince as Maekar, Daeron II's youngest son.

quote: Prince Aerys was bookish and Prince Rhaegel mad, meek, and sickly.
why: Characterization of Daeron II's middle sons, who did not attend the tourney — Rhaegel described as mad, meek, and sickly.

quote: By which you mean he'd sooner ride a whore than a horse," the first man said.
why: Baelor and Maekar quarrel over Prince Daeron's fitness for the lists; Daeron (eighteen) is called out as a drunkard.

quote: Ser Arlan, he told me you were the soul of chivalry, and that one day the Seven Kingdoms would be safe in your hands.
why: Dunk recalls Ser Arlan's praise of Baelor Breakspear as a future king, spoken to Baelor's face.

quote: Daeron is a sot, Aerion is vain and cruel, the third son was so unpromising they gave him to the Citadel to make a maester of him, and the youngest
why: Raymun Fossoway's rundown of Maekar's four sons — Daeron, Aerion, Aemon, and Aegon ("the youngest") — the sentence interrupted by Egg bursting in.

quote: "Prince Aerion Brightflame," a herald called, "of the Red Keep of King's Landing, son of Maekar Prince of Summerhall of House Targaryen, grandson to Daeron the Good, the Second of His Name, King of the Andals, the Rhoynar, and the First Men, and Lord of the Seven Kingdoms."
why: Herald's formal recitation places Aerion in the line of Maekar and Daeron II.

quote: his blood is the blood of Aegon the Conqueror and the Young Dragon and Prince Aemon the Dragonknight
why: Valarr's storied bloodline enumerated — Aegon the Conqueror, the Young Dragon, and Aemon the Dragonknight — as Dunk weighs challenging him.

quote: "Aegon," he repeated. "Of course it would be Aegon. Like Aegon the Dragon. How many Aegons have been king?"
why: Dunk connects Egg's true name to the royal line of Aegons, setting up the count of four kings named Aegon.

quote: I am the King's Hand and the heir to the throne, my word carries some weight. But so does my brother's. The risk is there.
why: Baelor states his own authority as Hand of the King and heir, weighed against Maekar's, ahead of Dunk's trial.

quote: "The blood of the dragon," Dunk said. "Silver-gold hair and purple eyes, everyone knows that." Thick as a castle wall, Dunk.
why: Dunk names the canonical Targaryen coloring — silver-gold hair and purple eyes — as common knowledge, moments after learning Egg's true identity.

quote: the Targaryens were the blood of the dragon, and their ends were writ in flame
why: Targaryen funeral custom stated: cremation rather than burial or burial at sea, tied to their draconic bloodline.

quote: Baelor of House Targaryen, Prince of Dragonstone, Hand of the King, Protector of the Realm, and heir apparent to the Iron Throne of the Seven Kingdoms of Westeros, went to the fire in the yard of Ashford Castle on the north bank of River Cockleswent.
why: Baelor Breakspear's full titles recited at his death and cremation.

quote: The king is old. When he dies, Valarr will climb the Iron Throne in place of his father.
why: With Baelor Breakspear dead, succession shifts to his son Valarr as the aged Daeron II's heir.

quote: Their oath forbids them to harm a prince of the blood. Fortunately, I am such.
why: Baelor exploits the Kingsguard oath forbidding harm to a prince of the blood, since he himself is one.

quote: The summers have been shorter since the last dragon died, and the winters longer and crueler.
why: Seasonal lore tying the death of the last dragon to shortened summers and lengthened, crueler winters.

quote: I dreamed of you and a dead dragon, you see. A great beast, huge, with wings so large they could cover this meadow. It had fallen on top of you, but you were alive and the dragon was dead.
why: Daeron's true, prophetic dream of Dunk beneath a dead dragon — a Targaryen prophetic-dreaming hook that pays off when Baelor (a dragon) dies to save Dunk.

quote: A falling star brings luck to him who sees it, Dunk thought.
why: Dunk's own luck-omen belief, seeded the night before the tourney, tied to the green falling star he witnesses.

quote: His hand trembled as he pointed a finger. "You stay away from me, do you hear? You stay well away."
why: The drunk lordling (later revealed as Prince Daeron) claims a dream of Dunk and warns him off — the prophetic-dream hook planted at the inn.

quote: His dream was wrong, then. The dead dragon. Unless Aerion died. He didn't though, did he?
why: Dunk's callback to Daeron's dream after Baelor's death, weighing whether the prophecy pointed to Aerion or to Baelor.

quote: The lamb was as good as any he had ever eaten, and the duck was even better, cooked with cherries and lemons and not near as greasy as most.
why: Load-bearing inn-meal description marking Dunk's first taste of what he calls 'what it means to be a knight.'

quote: There's good lamb, roasted with a crust of herbs, and some ducks my son shot down. Which will you have?
why: The innkeep's offer of lamb and duck — the meal Dunk hasn't had in half a year.

quote: Dunk could smell woodsmoke, and bacon as well.
why: Tourney-ground food atmosphere — woodsmoke and bacon on the morning air among the pavilions.

quote: We'll buy oats for the horses and fresh bread for ourselves. Maybe a bit of cheese as well, they were selling good cheese at one of the stalls.
why: Tourney-market fare Dunk plans to buy for himself and Egg.

quote: Behind it, two servants were basting a goat with honey and herbs over a small cookfire.
why: Fossoway pavilion hospitality: a goat basted with honey and herbs over a cookfire.

quote: Dunk bought a sackful of foodstuffs, so they were able to break their fast on goose eggs; fried bread, and bacon, but when the food was cooked he found he had no appetite.
why: Tourney-morning breakfast of goose eggs, fried bread, and bacon — eaten without appetite before the first day's contests.

quote: There was hot bread and honey, a bowl of pease porridge, a skewer of roast onions and well-charred meat.
why: The prisoner's meal brought to Dunk's cell — hot bread and honey, pease porridge, and skewered meat.

quote: "Cry for quarter and admit your guilt, and perhaps I'll only claim a hand and a foot. Oh, and those teeth, but what are a few teeth? A man like you can live years on pease porridge."
why: Aerion's taunt names pease porridge as a cripple's fare, threatening to maim Dunk.

quote: Daeron never slept in a ditch, I'll wager," Dunk said, very quietly, "and all the beef that Aerion ever ate was thick and rare and bloody, like as not.
why: Dunk contrasts the princes' rich fare (thick, rare, bloody beef) with the hard salt beef of a hedge knight's road, refusing Summerhall.

quote: "There's food as well, if you're hungry," Raymun said negligently as he held the flap for Dunk. A brazier of coals lit the interior and made the air pleasantly warm.
why: Raymun Fossoway's pavilion hospitality — food and warmth offered to Dunk, which he feels uneasy accepting since he cannot return it.

quote: "Pour Ser Duncan a cup of the sweet Dornish red, Aegon," the prince commanded. "Try not to spill it on him, you've done him sufficient ill already."
why: Baelor's solar hospitality to Dunk before delivering the trial choice — wine poured by his own nephew Egg.

quote: There's good lamb, roasted with a crust of herbs, and some ducks my son shot down. Which will you have?
why: Commercial inn hospitality — Dunk buys a proper meal and ale, his first in half a year.

quote: "There, you see, your matter is attended to, and the wine awaits," said Raymun. "It's an Arbor vintage, too. How can you refuse me?"
why: Raymun Fossoway presses hospitality (an Arbor vintage) on Dunk in Ser Steffon's gold pavilion, which Dunk feels he cannot properly return.

quote: "A knight who remembered his vows," the smith said.
why: Steely Pate's answer for why the smallfolk bless and cheer Dunk on his way to the trial of seven.

quote: the fierce three-headed dragon of House Targaryen seemed to spread its wings, breathing scarlet fire
why: Royal banner arrival set-piece as the Targaryen party reaches Ashford with a Kingsguard escort.

quote: an oaken shield with a scarred metal rim, bearing the sigil of Ser Arlan of Pennytree: a winged chalice, silver on brown
why: Ser Arlan of Pennytree's sigil, inherited by Dunk on the shield he carries to pass as a knight.

quote: their silver-gold hair and violet eyes set them apart from common men
why: Targaryen Valyrian features named as the identity marker — the concealed-identity key running through the whole D&E cycle.

quote: He was the first Lord Dondarrion, so he took for his arms a forked purple lightning bolt, on a black field powdered with stars.
why: Origin tale of House Dondarrion's sigil, as Dunk recounts it to Ser Manfred Dondarrion.

quote: "Sweet lady," said Florian, "all men are fools, and all men are knights, where women are concerned."
why: Line from the Florian-and-Jonquil puppet play performed at Ashford.

quote: watching a puppet show about Nymeria, the warrior queen with the ten thousand ships
why: Merchants'-row puppet show depicting Nymeria staged among the tourney entertainments.

quote: It came across the narrow sea with the Andals and their seven gods. In any trial by combat, the accuser and accused are asking the gods to decide the issue between them.
why: Baelor explains the trial of seven's Andal origin and religious logic.

quote: I remember, when I was little, he used to come into my bedchamber at night and put his knife between my legs. He had too many brothers, he'd say, maybe one night he'd make me his sister, then he could marry me. He threw my cat in the well too.
why: Egg's account of Aerion's childhood cruelty toward him, establishing Aerion as "quite the monster."

quote: Even by lantern light, the sunset colors were rich and bright, the tree tall and strong and noble. The falling star was a bright slash of paint across the oaken sky.
why: Tanselle's repainted shield sigil for Dunk — an elm on a sunset field with a falling star, replacing Ser Arlan's winged chalice.

quote: "Wine." The voice had a hollow metallic ring to it. "Not oil, that will kill him, boiling wine. I'll send Maester Yormwell to have a look at him when he's done tending my brother."
why: Baelor prescribes boiling wine (not boiling oil) for Dunk's wound — maester's-craft treatment lore.

quote: "A hedge knight is the truest kind of knight, Dunk," the old man had told him, a long long time ago.
why: Ser Arlan's creed on hedge knighthood and protecting the weak, recalled by Dunk in his cell.

quote: One fell as he was watching, a bright green streak that flashed across the black and then was gone.
why: A green falling star witnessed by Dunk the first night at Ashford, seeding the tourney's luck-omen thread.

quote: The youngest boy was with him as well. They left Summerhall together but never reached Ashford.
why: Raymun's report that Maekar's youngest son (Egg) is missing with Daeron — foreshadowing the boy's concealed identity as Aegon.

quote: "To warn you of what you face," Daeron said. "My father has commanded the Kingsguard to fight with him."
why: Maekar commands the Kingsguard to fight for Aerion in the trial of seven, escalating the stakes against Dunk.

quote: I shall be more than a knight before this day is done. Lord Fossoway. I like the sound of that.
why: Ser Steffon Fossoway betrays Dunk's side for the promised reward of being raised to Lord Fossoway.

quote: Prince Valarr has the honor to be one of Lady Ashford's champions," explained Ser Roland, "and two of his cousins mean to challenge.
why: Valarr, eldest son of Baelor Breakspear, named as one of Lady Ashford's tourney champions.

quote: It would not be fitting for us to ride against those we are sworn to protect," answered Ser Donnel, red of hair and beard.
why: The Kingsguard oath stated directly: sworn to protect the royal family, not to ride against those under their protection.

quote: There has not been a trial of seven for more than a hundred years, do you know that?
why: Ser Lyonel Baratheon notes the rarity of the trial of seven, last held over a century before.
